core_intuitions: The verification/transformation asymmetry: restyling text is easier than writing it, and verifying an answer is easier than generating it — this asymmetry is the engine that lets cheap compute be converted into scarce high-quality tokens.

The grounding spectrum: the further synthetic text departs from a human-written source (rephrase -> knowledge-augment -> prompt-seed -> free-gen), the more value it can add AND the more hallucination risk it carries.

Model collapse is caused by recursive REPLACEMENT, not by synthetic data per se: in 'replace' the fitted variance decays like (1-1/n)^t and the tails vanish; in 'accumulate' the retained real data re-anchors the distribution each generation and the decay halts.

Over-generate, filter hard: because correctness can be verified objectively (math/code), sampling K traces and keeping only verified-correct ones gives high coverage even from an unreliable teacher (1-(1-p)^K), which is why post-training data can be almost fully synthetic while pretraining mixes cannot.

Verification is a cheapest-first ladder: order filters so junk is discarded before paying for expensive checks, and decontamination against every reported benchmark is mandatory because generators leak memorized eval items.

existing.name: syndata-grounding-spectrum
existing.issue: In both themes the card TITLE (which wraps to two lines, e.g. 'Rephrase / restyle (WRAP)', 'Knowledge-augmented') overlaps the parenthetical subtitle and the body paragraph beneath it — the text lines collide and are hard to read. The bottom connector captions ('grounded — every fact traceable to a real doc', 'seeded by a doc but reformatted') also crowd and nearly overlap. The core structure — a low-risk-to-high-risk gradient arrow over four grounding stages — is correct and worth keeping.
existing.improvement: Rebuild each card as a clean vertical stack with dedicated, non-overlapping bands and generous line-height: (1) stage title, (2) one-line method subtitle, (3) 2-3 line body — measure text height and size the card to fit rather than overprinting. Shorten the body copy so it never runs into the title. Widen cards or reduce font so titles don't wrap into the subtitle. Anchor each bottom caption directly under its own card center on a single line (abbreviate so none wraps into a neighbor). Keep the theme-safe green->orange gradient arrow and the 'more departure = more value AND more risk' bottom label.

existing.name: syndata-verification-ladder
existing.issue: In both themes the numbered-stage TITLE (e.g. 'Format / length & language ID', 'Quality classifier') overlaps the description text below it inside every box — lines collide. There is also a stray black dot in the lower-left of box 1 (a rogue mark). The cheapest->most-expensive ordering, the five stages, the dropped-category callouts, and the final 'Clean shard kept' node are all good and should stay.
existing.improvement: Separate each box into stacked, padded lines — stage number + title on their own lines, then method, then action — sized so nothing overlaps. Remove the stray black dot. Optionally reinforce the funnel intuition by adding a thin 'records remaining' volume bar that visibly shrinks stage to stage (illustrative small integers only, no invented benchmark counts), making concrete that later, costlier stages process fewer items. Keep the cheapest->most-expensive top axis and the dropped-junk callouts under each stage.

new_figures.name: syndata-collapse-replace-vs-accumulate
new_figures.concept: Two side-by-side generational chains of a fitted Gaussian: REPLACE narrows toward a spike as variance decays like (1-1/n)^t; ACCUMULATE holds its width because retained real data re-anchors it.
new_figures.rationale: This is the central mathematical intuition of the chapter and has NO figure. The whole safety argument — 'collapse is caused by recursive replacement, not synthetic data' — rests on FEELING the difference between the two regimes. A single frame showing overlaid generations narrowing on one side and holding on the other makes the (1-1/n)^t decay and the real-data-as-anchor idea click instantly.
new_figures.placement: A toy model of collapse: the shrinking Gaussian
new_figures.brief: A two-panel figure sharing one horizontal value axis per panel. LEFT panel titled 'REPLACE — fit only on last generation's samples'. RIGHT panel titled 'ACCUMULATE — fit on original real data + all synthetic so far'. In EACH panel draw a family of ~5 bell curves representing generations t = 0, 5, 10, 15, 20, all centered on the same mean, distinguished by opacity/line-weight (gen 0 the boldest/darkest, later generations progressively lighter) with tiny 't=0','t=20' labels so the direction of time is unmistakable. LEFT: the curves get progressively TALLER and NARROWER (variance shrinking) — the latest curve is a thin tall spike collapsing onto the mean; annotate with a small callout 'tails vanish; variance -> 0'. RIGHT: all curves stay essentially the SAME width, overlapping nearly on top of each other; annotate 'real data re-anchors width each generation'. Between or above the two panels place the governing relation as a clean label: replace-regime expected variance ~ sigma^2 (1 - 1/n)^t decays; accumulate halts the decay. Add a small anchor motif on the RIGHT panel only — e.g. a small anchor icon or a bold 'real data' baseline curve that stays fixed — to visually explain WHY it holds. A one-line bottom caption: 'Collapse is recursive REPLACEMENT, not synthetic data itself.' Theme-safe: use one hue for the replace family and a second for the accumulate family (no hardcoded reds/greens that break in dark mode; rely on the palette variables). Only illustrative labels, invent no benchmark numbers; the (1-1/n)^t form and small integer generation labels are the only quantities. Everything must fit the viewBox with clear reading order left-to-right, no curve labels overlapping.

new_figures.name: syndata-overgenerate-filter-hard
new_figures.concept: Rejection-sampling fine-tuning: sample K chain-of-thought traces per problem, verify each, discard the wrong ones, keep verified-correct traces — with the coverage 1-(1-p)^K rising steeply in K.
new_figures.rationale: This is the foundational intuition behind Recipe 4 and behind 'why post-training datasets can be almost fully synthetic'. It has no figure. Readers need to SEE that verification turns an unreliable teacher into a high-precision data source: even a ~40%-correct teacher covers nearly all solvable problems at K=8. The pairing of the sample-and-filter funnel with the coverage curve makes the verification asymmetry concrete.
new_figures.brief: A composite figure in two linked halves. LEFT half — the sample-and-filter mechanic for ONE problem: at top a single 'Problem (known gold answer)' node; from it fan out K (~8) parallel 'trace' cards labeled 'CoT trace'. Each trace ends in an extracted final answer that is checked against the gold answer by a small 'verify' gate: mark the majority as WRONG (crossed out / dimmed, e.g. 5 of 8) and a minority as CORRECT (checkmark, highlighted, e.g. 3 of 8). Funnel the CORRECT traces down into a 'kept: verified (problem -> correct trace)' SFT bucket; show the WRONG traces dropping away. Add a tiny note 'cap per problem + keep DIVERSE correct traces' near the bucket. RIGHT half — the payoff curve: a small plot of coverage = 1-(1-p)^K on the y-axis (0 to 1) versus K on the x-axis (1..8+), drawn as a steeply rising, saturating curve. Mark the illustrative point at K=8 with a dashed guide showing coverage near the top for a modest per-attempt success p (label it 'p~0.4 teacher, K=8 -> ~0.98 covered' as an ILLUSTRATIVE example, using only these small round numbers that appear in the chapter text, inventing nothing new). Caption tying them together at the bottom: 'Over-generate, filter hard: verification converts an unreliable generator into a high-precision dataset.' Theme-safe palette variables only; correct = success hue, wrong = muted/neutral, no hardcoded colors. Keep all trace cards legible and non-overlapping; clear top-to-bottom then left-to-right reading order; everything inside the viewBox.

summary: The chapter is concept-rich but figure-poor for its two most important mechanisms. Both existing figures (grounding spectrum, verification ladder) are structurally sound but share a title/body text-overlap defect in both themes (plus a stray dot in the ladder) — REFINE both to fix legibility. Two foundational intuitions have no figure and should be added: (1) model collapse replace-vs-accumulate as side-by-side shrinking/holding Gaussians — the central math of the chapter; and (2) over-generate/filter-hard rejection sampling with the 1-(1-p)^K coverage curve — the engine of Recipe 4 and of 'why post-training data can be nearly all synthetic'. Both proposed as static single-frame infographics; no animation is essential.
